votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, using abs() and modulo 10 to compute the product of the unit digits of the absolute values.
The Python code correctly translates the Coq specification, using abs() and modulo 10 to compute the product of the unit digits of the absolute values of the inputs.
The Python code correctly translates the Coq specification, using abs and % 10 to compute the product of the unit digits of the absolute values.

The Python code directly mirrors the Coq specification. problem_97_pre always returns True (matching the Coq Prop True), and problem_97_spec checks if r equals the product of the unit digits of the absolute values of a and b, using the same formula: (abs(a) % 10) * (abs(b) % 10). Python's abs() and % operator on integers behave the same as Coq's Z.abs and mod for this purpose.

Python's abs and % operators on integers behave the same as Coq's Z.abs and mod for this purpose.

Both define r as the product of the unit digits of the absolute values of a and b; precondition is always true. Python’s abs and % 10 match Coq’s Z.abs and mod 10 with modulus 10.

Both define the precondition as always true and specify r as the product of the unit digits of the absolute values of a and b, using modulo 10. Python ints and % behavior with abs match Coq Z and mod here.
Both accept all integers and compute the product of the unit digits using absolute values; Python’s abs and % 10 matches Coq’s Z.abs mod 10 for all integers.
